title: Fine-Tuning a Reranker
concept: Adapting a reranking model on domain-specific relevance judgments for further accuracy gains.

exercises.prompt: How does a latency budget affect our choice of candidates in a reranking pipeline?

options.text: It determines the maximum number of candidate documents (top-K) we can afford to rerank.
It determines the physical distance between the vector database and the user.
It limits the vocabulary size of the LLM parser.

exercises.explain: The latency budget dictates how many candidates (top-K) we can afford to send to the computationally expensive reranker model without slowing down the application.

cards.title: Beyond General Knowledge
cards.body: Out-of-the-box rerankers are great at general knowledge, but they often stumble on specialized domains like legal documents, medical files, or company-specific codebases. Fine-tuning teaches the model your specific language.

cards.title: Fine-Tuning a Reranker
cards.body: Fine-tuning a reranker involves training a pre-trained model on your own domain data, using positive and negative query-document pairs, so it learns the exact nuances of your industry's terminology.

cards.title: The Specialized Grading Key
cards.body: Think of a general-domain reranker like a smart English teacher. If you ask them to grade essays on medical surgery, they need a specialized crash course (fine-tuning) with surgical grading keys to know which medical terms make an essay truly accurate.

cards.title: Domain Boost in Action
cards.body: A medical search engine fine-tunes its reranker using past search logs. It trains the model to rank clinical trials higher than generic lifestyle blog posts when doctors search for rare disease terms.

cards.title: The Data Myth
cards.body: People think fine-tuning a reranker requires millions of documents. In reality, you can see massive accuracy gains with just a few hundred to thousands of high-quality, domain-specific query-document relevance judgments.

cards.title: Generic vs. Fine-Tuned
cards.body: See how fine-tuning changes how a reranker views technical documents compared to a generic out-of-the-box model.

left.text: Confuses specialized terms with common synonyms, ranking superficial matches higher.

right.text: Recognizes exact domain relevance, placing precise technical answers at the very top.

exercises.prompt: Why is it faster to fine-tune an existing reranker than to train a new one from scratch?

options.text: It needs to learn English grammar from scratch.
It already has general language skills and just needs to learn domain-specific relevance.
Fine-tuning changes the vector database architecture completely.
Pre-trained rerankers cannot be customized at all.

exercises.explain: A pre-trained reranker already understands grammar and general relationships, so it only needs a smaller, specialized dataset to adapt to your domain's specific vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Fine-tuning a reranker helps it correctly rank documents that use highly specialized internal company jargon.
exercises.explain: Fine-tuning specializes the model's weights to rank documents on your specific topics, resulting in much higher search precision for domain-specific queries.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of the training process.
exercises.sentence: We train rerankers using contrastive learning to teach the model to score positive documents higher than negative ones.
exercises.explain: Contrastive learning is the training technique where the model learns to push relevant documents closer to the query while pushing irrelevant ones further away.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes a standard engineering approach to fine-tuning a reranker?

options.text: "To fine-tune a Cross-Encoder reranker, we construct training triplets containing a query, a positive document, and a hard negative document to force the model to learn fine-grained boundaries."
"Fine-tuning is achieved by adjusting the learning rate of the LLM generation head so that it outputs better summaries of search results."
"We optimize the reranking process by running a localized matrix factorization across all vector shards simultaneously."

exercises.explain: Option B mimics typical generative AI output describing generic search metrics rather than direct reranker training setup, and Option C is incorrect system jargon. Option A correctly captures the engineering reality of using hard negatives (similar but irrelevant docs) to fine-tune a Cross-Encoder reranker.

exercises.prompt: You want to fine-tune a pre-trained Cross-Encoder reranker to sort highly technical legal documents. Which dataset structure and goal is most appropriate for this task?

options.text: Provide triplets of (Query, Relevant Doc, Irrelevant Doc) to teach the model to rank the relevant doc higher.
Train the model on generic Wikipedia articles using unsupervised language modeling.
Only provide positive documents so the model learns to output high scores for everything.
Use a simple keyword counter without any query context.

exercises.explain: Fine-tuning requires triplets (Query, Positive Document, Negative Document) to teach the model to score the positive document higher than the negative one, steering it toward domain-specific definitions of relevance.
